notes.anchor: on your departure from your province the highest praise and the highest goodwill of the province escorted you
notes.note: Cassius had served as proquaestor in Syria after the death of Crassus and, left in effective command, repulsed a Parthian invasion under Pacorus near Antioch in 51 BC; that is the 'recent victory' Cicero congratulates.

notes.anchor: this recent victory of yours will make your arrival a brilliant one
notes.note: A successful commander returning to Rome could expect an honorific reception and possibly a thanksgiving; Cicero urges Cassius to hasten home while the glory of his Parthian success is fresh.

notes.anchor: no extension of time be given me for this province, which both the Senate and the people willed to be annual
notes.note: Cicero's command in Cilicia was legally fixed at one year; only a fresh senatorial decree could prolong it, and he repeatedly asks his friends at Rome to block any such extension.

M. Iunius Brutus, the future tyrannicide, closely linked to Cassius (who would later marry Brutus's half-sister Junia Tertia); the two are already a pair in Cicero's mind years before the conspiracy of 44 BC.
